content.text: Internal Rate Card Design for Claude Services
When multiple teams share a single Anthropic API account, the platform team acts as an internal broker: it pays the provider bill and charges internal teams based on their usage. An

defines the prices that internal teams are charged — set above provider list prices to recover platform overhead and maintain a reserve buffer for price changes. Without a rate card, cost attribution is imprecise and optimization incentives are absent.

attrs.alt: Internal rate card design: provider pricing to team charges via markup tiers

content.text: A rate card has three layers: the provider list price (the floor), the overhead markup (recovery of platform costs), and a reserve markup (buffer for provider price changes). Internal teams pay the sum of all three layers.
Recover logging, monitoring, auth, FinOps team time
Buffer for provider price increases; refunded quarterly if unused
What internal teams are charged per million tokens

The reserve markup is not profit — it is a working capital buffer. When Anthropic raises prices mid-quarter, the reserve covers the gap without requiring an emergency budget reallocation. Refund unused reserve to teams at quarter-end to maintain trust in the rate card model.

import dataclasses
from typing import Optional

# Provider list prices (USD per million tokens)
PROVIDER_PRICES = {
    "claude-sonnet": {"input": 3.00, "output": 15.00,
                      "cache_write": 3.75, "cache_read": 0.30},
    "claude-haiku":  {"input": 0.25, "output": 1.25,
                      "cache_write": 0.30, "cache_read": 0.03},
    "claude-opus":   {"input": 15.00, "output": 75.00,
                      "cache_write": 18.75, "cache_read": 1.50},
}

@dataclasses.dataclass
class RateCardEntry:
    """Internal rate card entry for one model x tier combination."""
    service_name: str          # e.g. "standard", "optimized", "haiku"
    model: str                 # base model
    overhead_markup: float     # fraction added for infra/monitoring recovery
    reserve_markup: float      # fraction added as price change buffer
    cache_discount_pass_through: bool  # pass cache savings to teams?

    def internal_price(self, token_type: str) -> float:
        """Compute the internal rate for a token type (per million tokens)."""
        provider = PROVIDER_PRICES[self.model]
        base = provider[token_type]
        markup = 1.0 + self.overhead_markup + self.reserve_markup
        if self.cache_discount_pass_through and token_type == "cache_read":
            # Pass cache read savings directly to teams; still apply overhead markup
            return base * (1.0 + self.overhead_markup)
        return base * markup

    def price_sheet(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "service": self.service_name,
            "model": self.model,
            "input_per_M":       round(self.internal_price("input"), 4),
            "output_per_M":      round(self.internal_price("output"), 4),
            "cache_write_per_M": round(self.internal_price("cache_write"), 4),
            "cache_read_per_M":  round(self.internal_price("cache_read"), 4),
            "total_markup_pct":  round((self.overhead_markup + self.reserve_markup) * 100, 1),
        }


RATE_CARD = [
    RateCardEntry(
        service_name="standard",
        model="claude-sonnet",
        overhead_markup=0.20,
        reserve_markup=0.10,
        cache_discount_pass_through=False,
    ),
    RateCardEntry(
        service_name="optimized",
        model="claude-sonnet",
        overhead_markup=0.20,
        reserve_markup=0.10,
        cache_discount_pass_through=True,   # teams see the cache savings
    ),
    RateCardEntry(
        service_name="haiku",
        model="claude-haiku",
        overhead_markup=0.20,
        reserve_markup=0.10,
        cache_discount_pass_through=True,
    ),
    RateCardEntry(
        service_name="opus",
        model="claude-opus",
        overhead_markup=0.20,
        reserve_markup=0.10,
        cache_discount_pass_through=False,
    ),
]

print(f"{'Service':<14} {'Input/M':>9} {'Output/M':>10} {'CacheR/M':>10} {'Markup':>8}")
print("-" * 56)
for entry in RATE_CARD:
    ps = entry.price_sheet()
    print(
        f"{ps['service']:<14} "
        f"${ps['input_per_M']:>7.4f}  "
        f"${ps['output_per_M']:>8.4f}  "
        f"${ps['cache_read_per_M']:>8.4f}  "
        f"{ps['total_markup_pct']:>6.1f}%"
    )
Service Tiers on the Rate Card

A single flat rate for all Claude usage ignores the fact that some workloads use prompt caching (which reduces effective input token cost) while others use expensive models unnecessarily. Define service tiers that map to real usage patterns.
Teams with prompt caching enabled; cache savings credited back
Classification, extraction, routing; 10-12x cheaper than Sonnet
Highest-capability tasks; approval required for new workloads

Passing cache savings through to the 'optimized' tier creates an optimization incentive: teams who invest in prompt caching see a lower internal bill immediately. Teams on 'standard' pay for uncached tokens whether or not they could cache — making the tier switch a clear cost lever.
Each API call records its service tier tag (standard, optimized, haiku, opus). The monthly charge computation reads tagged usage and applies rate card prices to produce line-item invoices.

Rate cards must be version-controlled. When a provider pricing change requires a rate card update, the old version must be preserved for backfilling historical charges and for auditing. Never overwrite the current rate card file — create a new versioned file.

# rate_card_v2025_q3.yaml
# Version-controlled rate card. Effective 2025-07-01.
# Previous version: rate_card_v2025_q2.yaml (archived, do not delete — needed for backfill)

version: "2025-Q3"
effective_date: "2025-07-01"
review_date: "2025-10-01"

provider_prices:
  # Snapshot of Anthropic list prices as of effective_date
  claude-sonnet:
    input_per_M:       3.00
    output_per_M:     15.00
    cache_write_per_M: 3.75
    cache_read_per_M:  0.30
  claude-haiku:
    input_per_M:       0.25
    output_per_M:      1.25
    cache_write_per_M: 0.30
    cache_read_per_M:  0.03

markup_policy:
  overhead_pct: 20   # Covers: logging, monitoring, auth proxy, FinOps team time
  reserve_pct:  10   # Price change buffer. Refunded quarterly if not consumed.

services:
  standard:
    model: claude-sonnet
    cache_pass_through: false
    description: "Default for all teams. No caching required."
  optimized:
    model: claude-sonnet
    cache_pass_through: true
    description: "For teams with enabled prompt caching. Cache savings credited."
  haiku:
    model: claude-haiku
    cache_pass_through: true
    description: "Low-cost tier for classification, extraction, and routing."
  opus:
    model: claude-opus
    cache_pass_through: false
    description: "Premium tier. Requires FinOps approval for new workloads."

changes_from_prior_version:
  - "Added 'optimized' service tier for cache-enabled workloads"
  - "Reserve markup reduced from 15% to 10% following stable Q2 pricing"

Store rate card YAML files in a dedicated repository alongside the charge computation code. A pull request to update the rate card triggers a CI pipeline that: (1) validates YAML schema, (2) runs a dry-run charge computation against last month's usage, (3) posts the delta (new charges vs old charges) to the PR for FinOps review before merging.
The rate card affects every team's budget. Changes require a governance process proportional to their impact.
: Schedule a rate card review for the first week of each quarter. Review actual provider prices, overhead costs, and reserve utilisation.
: Announce any rate increase to internal teams at least 30 days before the effective date so teams can adjust budgets and optimization plans.
: Document every change in the 'changes_from_prior_version' section so audit trails are self-contained.
: If a team is over-charged due to a rate card error, issue a credit in the following month's statement. Never modify historical charges — issue adjustments forward.
: Adding a new service tier (e.g., a future model) requires FinOps team sign-off on the markup policy before publishing to teams.

The reserve markup is charged to teams every month but may not all be consumed if provider prices stay flat. At quarter-end, reconcile the reserve: compute how much was collected, how much was needed to cover actual price changes, and refund the remainder to teams.

import dataclasses
from decimal import Decimal, ROUND_HALF_UP

@dataclasses.dataclass
class ReconciliationResult:
    team_id: str
    month: str
    internal_charges_usd: float    # what the rate card billed
    actual_provider_cost_usd: float  # what Anthropic actually charged
    overhead_collected_usd: float  # overhead portion of internal charges
    reserve_collected_usd: float   # reserve portion of internal charges
    reserve_utilized_usd: float    # how much of reserve was needed (price changes)
    reserve_refund_usd: float      # unused reserve to refund to teams


def reconcile_quarter(
    total_internal_charges: float,
    actual_provider_bill: float,
    overhead_markup_pct: float,
    reserve_markup_pct: float,
    price_change_delta: float = 0.0,  # extra cost due to mid-quarter price increase
) -> ReconciliationResult:
    """
    Compute end-of-quarter reconciliation.
    Unused reserve is refunded to teams proportional to their usage share.
    """
    total_markup_pct  = overhead_markup_pct + reserve_markup_pct
    provider_fraction = 1.0 / (1.0 + total_markup_pct)
    implied_provider  = total_internal_charges * provider_fraction

    overhead = total_internal_charges * (overhead_markup_pct / total_markup_pct) * (total_markup_pct / (1.0 + total_markup_pct))
    reserve  = total_internal_charges * (reserve_markup_pct  / total_markup_pct) * (total_markup_pct / (1.0 + total_markup_pct))
    utilized = min(price_change_delta, reserve)
    refund   = max(0.0, reserve - utilized)

    return ReconciliationResult(
        team_id="all-teams",
        month="Q3-2025",
        internal_charges_usd=round(total_internal_charges, 2),
        actual_provider_cost_usd=round(actual_provider_bill, 2),
        overhead_collected_usd=round(overhead, 2),
        reserve_collected_usd=round(reserve, 2),
        reserve_utilized_usd=round(utilized, 2),
        reserve_refund_usd=round(refund, 2),
    )

r = reconcile_quarter(
    total_internal_charges=130_000,
    actual_provider_bill=100_000,
    overhead_markup_pct=0.20,
    reserve_markup_pct=0.10,
    price_change_delta=2_500,   # provider raised prices mid-quarter; cost $2.5k extra
)
print(f"Total internal charges:  ${r.internal_charges_usd:,.2f}")
print(f"Overhead collected:      ${r.overhead_collected_usd:,.2f}")
print(f"Reserve collected:       ${r.reserve_collected_usd:,.2f}")
print(f"Reserve utilized:        ${r.reserve_utilized_usd:,.2f}")
print(f"Reserve to refund:       ${r.reserve_refund_usd:,.2f}")

Post the reconciliation result as a credit line in the following month's invoice rather than issuing a direct refund. This keeps the accounting within the internal billing system and avoids the overhead of inter-department wire transfers. Teams see the credit offset against their next month's charge.

Transparency builds trust. Publish the reconciliation calculation to all team leads — show what the reserve collected, what was consumed, and what is being refunded. A rate card where teams can verify the math is trusted; one that is opaque creates resentment.
Rate Card API for Programmatic Access

Engineering teams building cost-aware applications need to query the current rate card without hardcoding prices. A lightweight rate card API allows applications to fetch current rates at startup and cache them for the session.

from flask import Flask, jsonify, request
import yaml, datetime

app = Flask(__name__)

def load_current_rate_card() -> dict:
    with open("rate_cards/rate_card_current.yaml") as f:
        return yaml.safe_load(f)

@app.route("/api/v1/rate-card", methods=["GET"])
def get_rate_card():
    """Return the current rate card as JSON."""
    card = load_current_rate_card()
    return jsonify({
        "version":        card["version"],
        "effective_date": card["effective_date"],
        "review_date":    card["review_date"],
        "services":       card["services"],
        "markup_policy":  card["markup_policy"],
    })

@app.route("/api/v1/rate-card/estimate", methods=["POST"])
def estimate_charge():
    """
    Estimate the internal charge for a given token usage.
    Body: {"service": "standard", "input_tokens": 1000000, "output_tokens": 250000}
    """
    body    = request.json
    service = body.get("service", "standard")
    input_t = body.get("input_tokens", 0)
    output_t= body.get("output_tokens", 0)

    card = load_current_rate_card()
    # Compute prices from YAML (simplified)
    markup = 1.0 + card["markup_policy"]["overhead_pct"] / 100 + card["markup_policy"]["reserve_pct"] / 100
    provider = card.get("provider_prices", {}).get("claude-sonnet", {})
    input_rate  = provider.get("input_per_M", 3.00) * markup
    output_rate = provider.get("output_per_M", 15.00) * markup

    charge = (input_t / 1_000_000 * input_rate) + (output_t / 1_000_000 * output_rate)
    return jsonify({
        "service": service,
        "input_tokens": input_t,
        "output_tokens": output_t,
        "estimated_charge_usd": round(charge, 4),
        "rate_card_version": card["version"],
    })

Expose both a read endpoint (GET /api/v1/rate-card) and an estimate endpoint (POST /api/v1/rate-card/estimate). The estimate endpoint enables cost-aware request routing: an application can estimate the cost of a query before sending it and downgrade to the haiku tier if the estimate exceeds a threshold.
Single flat rate for all models
High-model usage subsidized by cheap-model users
Separate rate card entry per model/tier
Price increase mid-year forces emergency budget re-allocation
Include 5-15% reserve; refund quarterly if unused
Teams have no billing incentive to implement caching
Create an 'optimized' tier that credits cache savings
Version drift; no audit trail; change process unclear
YAML in version control with pull request review
Teams cannot plan; trust breaks down

An internal rate card defines per-token prices charged to internal teams, set above provider list prices by an overhead markup (15–25% to recover platform costs) and a reserve markup (5–15% as a price change buffer). Define service tiers per model and cache configuration to pass optimization incentives to teams: teams with prompt caching enabled pay the 'optimized' tier rate and see their cache savings credited. Version-control the rate card as a YAML file with an effective date and changelog. Govern changes with 30-day advance notice, quarterly reviews, and a CI pipeline that dry-runs the impact before any update merges.
